# **1.3 Sustainability**


## **Introduction**


Sustainability is a key concept in Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) within the International Baccalaureate (IB) syllabus. It involves understanding how to meet the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. This study note will delve into the principles, components, and real-world applications of sustainability, making it easier to grasp and apply these concepts.

## **Sustainability and Sustainable Development**


Environmental sustainability means using global resources at a rate that allows natural regeneration and minimizes damage to the environment.

Social sustainability focuses on the creation of structures and systems such as health, education and community to support human well-being. It focuses on the survival of societies and their culture.

Economic sustainability focuses on creating economic structures and systems to support the production and consumption of goods and services that will support human needs. Economic sustainability cannot be achieved without environmental sustainability.

## **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**


Sustainable development refers to meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. The United Nations has outlined 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) to address global challenges and achieve a better and more sustainable future for all.

<callout type="note">The SDGs provide a comprehensive framework for addressing sustainability on a global scale.</callout>

<callout type="tip">Learn some specific examples of sustainable schemes - for example, Curitiba has a sustainable transport program and a ‘green exchange’ whereby residents can exchange waste products for food or bus tickets.</callout>

****

![Image](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/fe2ece7d62206e4bbe38b65837ba4bf37f7d26cb-1024x653.png)

## **Environmental justice**


Environmental justice refers to the right of all people to live in a pollution-free environment and to equitable access to natural resources, regardless of issues such as race, gender, socioeconomic status and nationality. A significant example of environmental injustice is the Minamata Disease where economically underprivileged citizens (fishermen and their families) were significantly affected.

[Read more on Minimata Disease](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/minamata-disease#brief-history-of-the-disease)

## **Inequalities**


Inequalities in income, age, gender and cultural identity can lead to disparities in access to water, food and energy. Studies have shown coloured communities in the USA are more exposed to the harmful effects of air and noise pollution than their affluent counterparts. Studies have also shown that economically privileged families can combat the unavailability of resources due to their financial resources while economically underprivileged families cannot.

Environmental justice and inequalities need to be considered when governments and international bodies design and implement environmental policies and projects.

## **Sustainability indicators**


### **Ecological Footprint**


The ecological footprint represents the hypothetical area of land required by a society, group, or individual to fulfil all their resource needs and the assimilation of wastes. If the EF is greater than the area available to the population, the population is living unsustainably.

#### **Components of Ecological Footprint:**


* Carbon footprint (energy use)
* Food footprint (agricultural land)
* Housing footprint (built-up land)
* Goods and services footprint (resource consumption)

<callout type="example">A person who drives a fuel-efficient car, recycles, and uses renewable energy will have a smaller ecological footprint than someone who relies heavily on fossil fuels and generates a lot of waste.</callout>

<callout type="tip">You need to be able to explain the relationship between ecological footprint and sustainability.</callout>

### **Carbon footprint**


Carbon footprint measures the amount of greenhouse gases produced by our actions. It includes direct emissions, like fossil-fuel combustion due to manufacturing and transportation and indirect emissions, like the production of electricity associated with goods and services consumed. Calculating carbon footprint can help us adopt methods to make our lives and businesses (manufacturing, production and transportation) more sustainable.

## **The planetary boundaries model **


The planetary boundaries model describes nine planetary systems and processes that regulate and stabilize the Earth. Staying within these boundaries allows the continuation and development of the human race. Crossing these boundaries indicates irreversible environmental changes. The planetary boundaries are interdependent, meaning crossing one boundary means other boundaries are affected too.

#### **Strengths:**


1. Identifies science-based limits to human disturbance
2. Highlights the need to focus on more than climate change
3. Alerts the public and policymakers

#### **Limitations**


1. The model is a work in progress. The most recent update was done in 2023.
2. The focus on global boundaries may not be useful at the local or country level
3. Focuses only on ecological systems and does not consider the human dimension

[Read more here](https://www.stockholmresilience.org/research/planetary-boundaries.html)

![Image](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3ca83c940299c15d52a9ad0c43271a798e19c0dd-1620x695.png)

## **The doughnut economics model**


The doughnut economics model is a framework for creating a regenerative and distributive economy in order to meet the needs of all people within the means of the plant. The inner ring of the doughnut is the social foundation which ensures no one lacks life’s essentials like food and water. The outer ring is an ecological ceiling which ensures the nine planetary boundaries are not crossed. The space between these rings is the doughnut space where humanity can thrive safely. The model suggests that we need to move into the doughnut space which can be achieved by making economics regenerative and distributive. A regenerative economy means restructuring the economy to focus on making positive contributions to nature and society. A distributive economy focuses on sharing values and opportunities among all stakeholders in an equitable manner.

#### **Strengths:**


1. It includes both ecological and social elements, supporting the concept of environmental justice
2. It has become popular and is being used at different scales to support action on sustainability

#### **Limitations:**


1. The model is a work in progress
2. It advocates broad principles of regenerative and distributive practice but does not propose specific policies

[Read more here](https://doughnuteconomics.org/about-doughnut-economics)

![Image](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b0d9b96e9c2d8fcc7dd739dcf3e23f7696059f2b-600x562.jpg)

## **Circular economy (CE)**


The circular economy is a model that involves production and consumption involving sharing, reusing, repairing and recycling existing materials and products for as long as possible to reduce waste and overuse of resources. It has three principles: eliminating waste and pollution, circulating products and materials and regenerating nature.

The butterfly diagram from the Egram by Ellen MacArthur is a useful illustration of the CE.

![Image](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/30e87c6b074c4dbc0e005811eb54f9ecc1e4c042-2448x1656.png)

#### **Benefits of adopting CE:**


1. Reduces greenhouse emissions and waste by extending the product life cycle
2. Promotes sustainable consumer habits
3. Regeneration of natural systems

#### **Factors preventing the adoption of CE:**


1. Lack of environmental awareness by consumers and companies
2. Lack of regulations enforcing recycling
3. Technical and financial limitations

## **Conclusion**


Understanding sustainability in ESS involves grasping the intricate balance between environmental, economic, and social factors. The understanding of sustainability, circular economy, planetary boundaries, the doughnut economics model and the SDGs, and calculating sustainability indicators, we can work towards a more sustainable future. This knowledge not only helps in academic pursuits but also in making informed, responsible decisions in everyday life.
